game_name: Dune II: The Building of a Dynasty

design_philosophy.core_loop: Harvest spice from sand dunes → convert to credits via refinery → construct military units → defend base and destroy enemy bases → progress to next territory
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Resource management under time pressure with dual threats: enemy AI incursions and environmental hazards (sandworms, spice blooms, terrain decay)
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Command a Great House competing for planetary dominion; manage logistics of conquest while leveraging faction-specific military advantages
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Establishes the foundational RTS template through deliberate design choices: persistent fog-of-war revelation (exploration has permanent value), faction asymmetry enforced via unit availability restrictions (Harkonnen cannot build trikes; Ordos cannot build Atreides sonic tanks), technology tree gating that unlocks superior units through mission progression, and environmental mechanics (spice decay, concrete foundations) that penalize neglect. Player choice of House (Atreides/Harkonnen/Ordos) fundamentally shapes available unit roster and strategic options.

difficulty.continues_system: Mission-based progression; failure restarts single mission
difficulty.checkpointing: Missions are discrete objectives with completion requiring enemy base destruction or spice harvest targets
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: House selection determines unit availability and asymmetric power curves. Territory selection on world map provides limited choice of opponent (affects which enemy House is faced). Speedrun times range 2m to 40m+ indicating significant skill expression and optimization space.

progression.upgrade_system: Technology tree unlocks via mission completion; each mission grants authorization for improved unit types and super-weapons unique to chosen House. Captured enemy factories enable manufacturing of rival House units.
progression.unlock_mechanism: Mission success gates access to higher-tier units and structures. Nine territories must be conquered (in player-chosen order from 2-3 options per stage) before triggering endgame alliance vs Emperor's Sardaukar.
replayability_hooks: Three distinct Houses with unique unit rosters and strategic identities (Atreides/Harkonnen/Ordos)
Territory selection provides limited branching (choose from 2-3 options per mission stage)
Faction-specific introductory and endgame cutscenes differentiate narrative per House
27 total missions (9 per House) with varying enemy compositions and map layouts
Speedrun community demonstrates high optimization potential and varied routing strategies

world_building_techniques: Briefing cutscenes establish political context (Emperor Frederick IV's debt crisis forcing Houses to compete for spice governance)
House-specific introductory and endgame cinematics reflect 'very disparate world views'
Environmental storytelling via faction unit aesthetics (Harkonnen heavy armor/ordnance philosophy vs Atreides sonic technology vs Ordos nerve-gas subterfuge)
Sandbox mission objectives imply emergent narrative (player negotiates shifting alliances and territorial conquest)
Final mission forces temporary enemy alliance against player, suggesting geopolitical dynamics

boss_design.philosophy: Final endgame confrontation features combined remnant armies of two rival Houses plus Emperor's elite Sardaukar force (described as 'unplayable elite' with particularly powerful heavy infantry). Represents escalation from single-House opponents to multi-faction alliance.
notable_bosses: Emperor's Sardaukar (elite heavy infantry, introduced in final mission as ally-turned-combatant)

faction_indicators: Atreides: Access to Sonic Tank (signature technology), standard trike units, balanced military doctrine
Harkonnen: Devastator tank (heavy armor/ordnance), Quad vehicles (no trikes), aggressive resource-intensive approach
Ordos: Deviator tank (nerve-gas unit conversion mechanic), Raider trikes (faster than Atreides), subterfuge-oriented tactics
danger_signals: Spice bloom explosions (attempting to trigger hidden spice fields destroys units)
Sandworm marauding (environmental predator swallows vehicles/infantry; avoided by rocky terrain)
Structure decay over time (concrete foundations mitigate but do not prevent; neglect incurs repair costs)
Enemy base destruction missions (escalate from defensive harvesting to offensive conquest)
environmental_storytelling: Arrakis map shows spice concentration via orange coloration intensity (darker = richer fields, higher risk)
Rocky terrain as mandatory construction foundation contrasts with hostile sand dunes
Spice harvesters as mechanical lifeline (destruction forces economic collapse)
Territorial conquest via world map selection creates sense of expanding dominion
